coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared II fluorescent carbon dots for differential imaging of drug-resistant bacteria and dynamic monitoring of immune system defense against bacterial infection in vivo
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Weijian
Wu, Bingbing
Sun, Wen
Liu, Wenkai
Gu, Hua
Du, Jianjun
Fan, Jiangli
Peng, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: In vivo fluorescence imaging allows the accurate acquisition of dynamic biological information, which plays a vital role in the early diagnosis and treatment of bacterial infectious diseases. Herein, a NIR-II-emitting CD-based nanoprobe (CyCDs) is synthesized using Cy7-CH3 as the carbon source and 3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride as the surface passivator. Notably, the π-conjugated system and rigid planar structure of the carbon source can broaden the wavelength range of absorption and emission. The presence of amino groups enables CyCDs with a positive zeta potential (+0.32 mV), which can facilitate the interactions between CyCDs and bacteria. Interestingly, CyCDs can only stain the cell wall of drug-resistant bacteria, while normal bacteria have a bright fluorescent signal over all of the cell. This phenomenon enables the identification of drug-resistant bacteria in vitro. Furthermore, CyCDs can be used to monitor the interaction between the immune system and infected bacteria in vivo and guide the timely usage of antibiotics
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near-infrared II fluorescent carbon dots
Drug-resistant bacteria
NIR-II fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of second near-infrared emitting CDs
Culturing and imaging of different strains of bacterial cells with CyCDs
In vivo NIR II fluorescence imaging
NIR-I/II fluorescence imaging of bacteria in vitro
Real-time NIR-II fluorescence imaging of bacterial infection in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of second near infrared (NIR)-emitting carbon dots (CyCDs) by solvothermal processing.

Characterization of CyCDs. (A) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CyCDs (inset: HR-TEM). (B) The dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution of CyCDs. (C) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CyCDs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cy7-CH3 and CyCDs. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of CyCDs under 808 nm laser excitation. (F) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the CyCDs.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of drug-resistant bacteria (E. coli (ATCC35218, G−), A. baumannii and MR S. aureus). (B) Imaging mechanism of CyCDs in drug-resistant bacteria. (C) Confocal microscopy fluorescent images of 0.25% trypsin (C2) treated MR S. aureus incubated with CyCDs. (D) Fluorescence imaging of normal bacteria (E. coli (ATCC8739, G−), S. aureus, L. monocytogenes and S. agalactiae (G+)).

(A) NIR-II imaging (λex = 808 nm, power: 2 W, exposure time: 50, 100, 200, and 300 ms, filter: LP1100) of CyCDs in PBS buffer. (B) Corresponding NIR-II fluorescence intensity statistics of the NIR-II CD solution. (C) NIR-II images (λex = 808 nm, power: 2 W, exposure time: 100 ms) of the original supernatants and bacterial sediments (MR S. aureus and E. coli, 1012 cfu/mL). (D) Corresponding NIR-II fluorescence intensity statistics with different filters (LP900, LP1100 and LP1300, λex = 808 nm, power: 2 W, exposure time: 100 ms).

(A) NIR-II images of Female BALB/c mice at different times post-injection. (B)-(E) Inflammatory factors (IL-6, TNF-α, MCP-1 and CD11B) measured with enzyme link immunosorbent assay (ELISA; Exp.: Experimental group). (F) Giemsa staining for residual bacterial (Staphylococcus aureus) detection in skin tissues after 7 days. Scale bar, 50 μm.
(A) Confocal imaging and discrimination of bacteria by CyCDs. (B) Bacterial infection NIR II imaging in vivo based on CyCDs.

head.ce:title: Near-infrared II fluorescent carbon dots for differential imaging of drug-resistant bacteria and dynamic monitoring of immune system defense against bacterial infection in vivo

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Frontiers Science Center for Smart Materials Oriented Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals
Frontiers Science Center for Smart Materials Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Frontiers Science Center for Smart Materials Oriented Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Dalian University of Technology, Ningbo 315016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Dalian University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ningbo Institute of Dalian University of Technology, Ningbo 315016, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In vivo fluorescence imaging allows the accurate acquisition of dynamic biological information, which plays a vital role in the early diagnosis and treatment of bacterial infectious diseases. Herein, a NIR-II-emitting CD-based nanoprobe (CyCDs) is synthesized using Cy7-CH3 as the carbon source and 3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride as the surface passivator. Notably, the π-conjugated system and rigid planar structure of the carbon source can broaden the wavelength range of absorption and emission. The presence of amino groups enables CyCDs with a positive zeta potential (+0.32 mV), which can facilitate the interactions between CyCDs and bacteria. Interestingly, CyCDs can only stain the cell wall of drug-resistant bacteria, while normal bacteria have a bright fluorescent signal over all of the cell. This phenomenon enables the identification of drug-resistant bacteria in vitro. Furthermore, CyCDs can be used to monitor the interaction between the immune system and infected bacteria in vivo and guide the timely usage of antibiotics

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Diseases caused by pathogenic bacterial infections have high morbidity and mortality rates and pose a serious threat to human health [1–3]. Currently, antibiotic therapy is the main method to fight bacterial infections [4,5], but broader prescription and unnecessarily prolonged adoption of antibiotics lead to bacterial resistance, even resulting in the emergence of superbugs [6–8]. Furthermore, the symptoms of bacterial infections caused by drug-resistant bacteria are the same as those of normal bacteria, which can interfere with the rational use of antibiotics in clinical practice [9]. Therefore, after failure to identify bacterial species and a lack of proper antibiotics at an early stage, the infection can progress rapidly into a systemic disease, even leading to life-threatening conditions [10]. Thus, rapid and efficient bacterial identification is of great importance in clinical diagnosis and antibiotic usage.

A variety of methods have been designed for the identification of G+ and G−, or live and dead bacterial cells, including the standard plate count (SPC) [11,12], polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [13], gene sequencing identification techniques [14], gene microarray [6,15], fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) [16] and immunological techniques [17,18]. Although they have good selectivity and identification of bacteria, these methods cannot achieve the differentiation of drug-resistant bacteria from normal bacteria to date.

Recently, fluorescence technology has been demonstrated to be a simple and effective method to identify bacteria in vitro [14,19–22]. Hence, fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs), fluorescence dyes, and quantum dots have been widely used as fluorescence labels combined with highly specific recognition elements (bacteriophage, antibody, aptamer, and antimicrobial peptide) for the rapid detection of bacteria [1,2,17,23,24]. For example, Tang et al. used fluorescence (AIEgen)-labeled bacteriophages to specifically recognize host bacteria [25]. Antibiotic (vancomycin and daptomycin)-modified fluorescent nanoparticles (NPs) were also prepared for specific bacterial targeting [26–28]. Unfortunately, the long-term use of these methods can easily lead to the development of drug resistance [19]. Thus, attempts to discriminate between drug-resistant bacteria and normal bacteria based on fluorescence imaging are scarcely reported [15].

Compared to organic dyes, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials of size < 10 nm, have received considerable attention due to their superior optical properties, ease of synthesis, excellent biocompatibility, anti-photobleaching effects, and outstanding water solubility [4,5,19,29–33]. The reported CDs are not only able to distinguish bacterial viability by staining dead cells but have also been verified as a typical nano-photosensitizer for inactivation of bacteria, while bacteria do not easily develop drug resistance [5,19,34,35]. However, most of the CDs utilized short wavelength light (<600 nm) as the excitation source, which could cause severe photodamage to tissues [29]. Cyanine 7 dyes possess a strong near-infrared (NIR) absorption character and can realize NIR fluorescence imaging under a single wavelength excitation. However, they suffer from the aggregation-caused quenching effect and seriously undergo photobleaching during long-term imaging. An alternative strategy is to develop nanoscale formulations from cyanine dyes [4,19,36]. In our previous work, we synthesized real NIR-emitting CDs using carboxyl cyanine dyes for discriminative imaging and photoinactivation of pathogenic bacteria, in which the inherent conjugated electronic structure of dye molecules could be partly reserved [4]. Since fluorescence imaging in the second near-infrared (NIR-II) region (900–1700 nm) presents significantly reduced scattering losses and much deeper bio-tissues with higher spatial resolution [35–37], in this work, by introducing rigid 3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride as the surface passivator, we synthesized CyCDs (λem = 1160 nm) based on cyanine 7 dyes (Cy7-CH3) through a facile solvothermal route [4]. The presence of amino groups enabled CyCDs with a positive surface, which could bind to bacteria (negative surface) rapidly. To our surprise, CyCDs can only stain the cell wall of drug-resistant bacteria while staining normal bacterial cells overall (Scheme 1 ). During in vivo observation, the CyCDs displayed quick detection of bacteria at the infected sites and allowed a real-time investigation of the change in bacterial load, which provides novel insights into antibiotic treatment according to bacterial load.

3-Hydroxytyramine hydrochloride was purchased from Energy Chemical. Luria-Bertani liquid nutrient medium, Luria-Bertani solid medium powder, and phosphate buffered solution (PBS, pH 7.2–7.4, 0.01 mol/L) were purchased from Solarbio Life Science. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC6538), methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MR S. aureus, ATCC43300), Streptococcus agalactiae (GBS, ATCC 13813), Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC8739), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA, ATCC27853), Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC35218), and Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii, ATCC19606) strains were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and purchased from Shanghai Luyu Technology Co., Ltd. Mouse IL-6 ELISA KIT(SEKM-0007-48T), TNF-a ELISA KIT, and Mouse MCP-1 ELISA KIT(SEKM-0108) were purchased from Solarbio Life Science.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of second near-infrared emitting CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CyCDs were prepared by a facile solvothermal method [4]. In brief, 50 mg of Cy7-CH3 and 500 mg of 3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride were dissolved in 10 mL of methanol solution in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature (25 °C), violet suspensions were obtained. The resultant solution was then purified by silica column chromatography using a mixture of methanol and methylene chloride as eluents. Finally, the blue fraction eluted from the column was collected and dried under vacuum to obtain CyCDs.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were acquired using an electron microscope (TF30). The second near-infrared (NIR II) fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer. Absorption and emission spectra were obtained with an Agilent Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer G6860A and an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. CyCDs were well characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, iN10), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The nanosecond fluorescence lifetime was determined using the TCSPC technique on an F900 system. The zeta potential was recorded using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK). DSC and TGA curves were obtained from a TGA/DSC 3+ Instruments measurement. 1H NMR spectra were recorded on Bruker 400 spectrometer, where the chemical shift is based on tetramethyl silicon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Culturing and imaging of different strains of bacterial cells with CyCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A single colony of bacterial cells, viz., Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC6538), methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC43300), Streptococcus agalactiae, Escherichia coli (ATCC8739), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA, ATCC27853), Escherichia coli (ATCC35218), and Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii, ATCC19606), were picked from the solid LB agar plate and incubated in sterilized 10 mL of liquid LB broth medium. After incubation, the culture was kept at 37 °C overnight for the growth of bacterial cells in nutrient media. The optical density (O.D.) of the turbid culture at 600 nm was measured as 1.0. Subsequently, fresh bacterial suspensions were centrifuged (5000 rpm, 5 min) and washed twice with sterile PBS. Before imaging by confocal fluorescence microscopy (CLSM), all bacteria were stained with CyCDs (10 μg/mL) for 5 min in cell culture dishes. For CLSM imaging, 100× oil-immersion objectives were employed.

A typical MTT proliferation assay method was used to assess the in vitro cytotoxicity of CyCDs in 3T3 cells. The cells were cultured in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. The cells were incubated with CyCDs (0, 30, 60, 90, 120 and 150 µg/mL) for 12 h in the dark. Then, 100 µL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for another 4 h. After that, the MTT was replaced with DMSO (200 µL) in each well, and the absorbance at 570 nm and 630 nm was recorded by a microplate reader (SpectraMax M2e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo NIR II fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: All the animal experiments involved in this work were approved by the Animal Experiment Ethics Committee of Dalian University of Technology. Female BALB/c mice (8 weeks, 16–20 g) or Female BALB/c nude mice were purchased from Changsheng Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Changchun, China) [4,5]. For NIR II fluorescence imaging applications, the Female BALB/c mice were anesthetized with 5% chloral hydrate [37]. Then, the Female BALB/c mice were subcutaneously infected with 50 μL of Staphylococcus aureus labeled with CyCDs (50 μg/mL) on the back away from another at a bacterial concentration of 1.5 × 1012 cfu/mL [7,10]. Subsequently, the same amount of PBS was injected at another two sites as a control group [7,10]. After that, NIR-II fluorescence images were recorded at 1 min, 10 min, 5 h, 24 h, 1 day, 2 days, 3 days and 7 days post-infection. The excitation light source was an 808 nm laser. The light emitted from the mice was filtered by a 900, 1100, and 1300 nm long pass filter and finally collected by an InGaAs CCD camera (Suzhou NIR-Optics Co., Ltd., China) for NIR-II imaging.

The preparation of CyCDs can be readily accomplished by heating Cy7-CH3 and 3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride in a methanol solution at 180 °C for 4 h in an autoclave, followed by purification via silica column chromatography (Fig. 1 ), similar to our previous work [4].

As presented in Fig. 2 A and B, the obtained CyCDs showed a spherical-like structure with an average diameter of 3.11 nm were observed by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS), respectively. A well-resolved crystal lattice of the CyCDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm could be noted from the HR-TEM image (inset in Fig. 2A). The crystalline nature of CyCDs was further identified by XRD, as depicted in Fig. 2C. A broad peak is located at 21°, which is consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of graphitic-like carbon-based materials and suggests that the precursors were partially carbonized into CyCDs [29,31]. TGA analysis of CyCDs shows the higher stability of the structures formed than Cy-CH3 (Fig. S1 A and B). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results (Fig. S1C) indicated that CyCDs were mainly composed of C (80.82%), a proportion of O (15.08%) and an extremely low proportion of N (4.1%), which is in accord with the crystalline nature characterized by XRD and HR-TEM. Moreover, the surface charge of CyCDs was also investigated by zeta potential analysis, demonstrating the positive charge nature on the surface (+0.32 mV, Fig. S2). In Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the CyCDs, the absorption bands at 3340, 1552 and 1436 cm−1 were corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of N-H, and the stretching vibration of C-N, respectively, indicating that the CyCDs were terminated with rich amino groups on the surface (Fig. 2F) [38]. High-resolution N 1s spectra reveals that N is present in the forms of amino groups (399.5 eV), graphitic N (401.2 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.2 eV) in CyCDs (Fig. S1E), which indicates that N contributes to the construction of a carbon core and acts as functional passivated groups (amino group) on the surface of the CyCDs [39]. The positive surface charge (amino group) is critical for bacterial detection because the surface of bacteria is negatively charged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NIR-I/II fluorescence imaging of bacteria in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Given the above excellent fluorescence properties of CyCDs in the NIR-I/II regions, the NIR-I imaging performance for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MR S. aureus (G+)) staining was first examined. Due to the amino groups on the surface, the CyCDs could rapidly approach and interact with MR S. aureus (G+). Bacterial cell wall components contain phosphoic acids (negatively charged), so that the phosphate groups can bind to the amino group (Fig. S6). As depicted in Fig. S7, the cell surface of MR S. aureus bacteria could be quickly lit-up upon coincubation with CyCDs for 1 min under confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). With increasing coincubation time, the fluorescence signal of the bacteria was significantly enhanced but still on the cell surface of MR S. aureus. The same phenomenon was observed with other drug-resistant bacteria (E. coli, ATCC35218, A. baumannii, G−) after being mixed with CyCDs (Fig. 3 A). Meanwhile, CyCDs quickly penetrated normal bacteria (E. coli, ATCC8739, G−), S. aureus, L. monocytogenes and S. agalactiae (G+) and emitted strong fluorescence signals (Fig. 3D). These results indicated that CyCDs-based fluorescence imaging could realize the specific recognition of drug-resistant bacteria.

A reasonable explanation is that a portion of the CyCDs could enter the drug-resistant bacteria and then be rapidly excreted from the bacteria by efflux pumps (Fig. 3B). The cell structures and/or composition of drug-resistant bacteria and normal bacteria are completely different [19]. In contrast to normal bacteria, drug-resistant bacteria contain a special protein transport system (efflux pump), which can excrete drugs or non-nutrient substances from the bacteria (Fig. 3B). To understand the interaction between the CyCDs and drug-resistant bacteria, we used 0.25% trypsin to remove the surface protein from the MR S. aureus [14]. Surprisingly, the overall bacterial cells could be quickly lit-up after mixing with CyCDs under CLSM (Fig. 3C2). This result proves that the CyCDs can enter the interior of resistant bacteria when the efflux pump system on the surface of resistant bacteria is disrupted.

To further realize NIR-II fluorescence imaging, in vitro imaging of CyCDs solution was first performed. As the concentration of CyCDs increased, the NIR-II fluorescence signals of solution increased accordingly (Fig. 4 A and B). However, it should be noted that NIR-II fluorescence signals can also be generated by adjusting the exposure time when the CyCDs have low concentrations, paving the way for bacterial detection and imaging in vivo. Then, the bacterial labeling efficiency of CyCDs was investigated through co-culturing with bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli, 1012 cfu/mL) for 5 min in vitro. Strong fluorescence signals could be detected in the bacterial sediments, while no fluorescent signal was present in the supernatants after centrifugation, demonstrating rapid and successful labeling of bacteria by CyCDs (Fig. 4C and D). Interestingly, after the emission light was filtered with an 1100 nm long-pass filter, the strongest fluorescent signal was observed in the bacterial sediments, suggesting that a redshift of emission peak of CyCDs maybe occurred after interacting with bacteria. Thus, it is proven that CyCDs could label bacteria with high efficiency in vitro, further providing a promising probe for bacterial detection in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time NIR-II fluorescence imaging of bacterial infection in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Before bioimaging applications, the biocompatibility of CyCDs was evaluated on 3T3 fibroblast cells via MTT assay. As the concentration of CyCDs increased, the cell survival rate of 3 t3 fibroblast cells reached almost 100% (Fig. S8A). As shown in Fig. S8B and 8C, it was found that the concentration of CyCDs had no significant effect on the growth of bacteria (E. coli and MR S. aureus), further revealing that CyCDs can be used as biocompatible fluorescent probes in vivo.

Encouraged by NIR-II fluorescence imaging of bacteria in vitro, we then performed NIR-II fluorescence imaging of bacterial infection (Staphylococcus aureus) in Female BALB/c mice in vivo. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A (a2-a8), fluorescence signals were rapidly observed at the site of infection, while the control group showed no fluorescence signals. First, an increase in the fluorescence signal area was observed from 10 min to 5 h and reached the first peak at 5 h post-injection, which reflected a surge in bacterial number due to proliferation (Fig. 5A(a4)). Then, the fluorescence signal area showed a decrease after 24 h injection, suggesting that the invading bacteria were attacked by the immune system, leading to a reduction in the bacterial load (Fig. 5A(a5)). Finally, in the absence of further treatment, the fluorescence signal and area increased from 48 h post-injection until the end of observation, suggesting a rage of bacterial load over time (Fig. 5A(a6-a8)). In addition, Giemsa staining was utilized to further identify the bacterial residue at the infected sites 7 days post-injection. As shown in Fig. 5F, significant bacteria remained in the PBS and experimental groups at the infected sites. Thus, the whole process of bacterial infection can be accurately monitored by NIR-II imaging in vivo. However, Female BALB/c nude mice with immunodeficiency were overwhelmed by bacterial infection and died at 7 days post-injection [7,10]. Thus, it is essential to identify the time of initiating and terminating treatment depending on the subsequent development of infection.

To further study the underlying interactions between bacterial infection and the immune system, analysis of several inflammatory factors (CD11B, IL-6, TNF-α and MCP-1) was performed with an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay to assess the changes in the immune and inflammatory responses in vivo (Fig. 5B-E). IL-6 plays an important role in the immune system and inflammation. As shown in Fig. 5B, IL-6 first showed an increase after 30 min and peaked from 5 h to 72 h post-injection, indicating that inflammation and the immune system were involved. A decrease in CD11B was detected from 5 h to 24 h, and these cells differentiated into macrophages due to the inflammatory response (Fig. 5E). Meanwhile, the relatively high expression of TNF-α (mainly secreted by mononuclear macrophages) and MCP-1 (mainly promoted macrophage migration) further verified the involvement of the innate system and the inflammatory response (Fig. 5C and 5D). Therefore, the immune response became strong from 5 h to 72 h post-injection but weakened after that, which was consistent with the recorded NIR II imaging of NIR II CD-labeled bacteria at the same points. The parallel control group did not show such a trend, suggesting that the variation trend of inflammatory factors determined the initiation of an immune response against bacterial infection. At the same time, we investigated the safety of CyCDs in vivo through hematoxylin-and-eosin staining of the major organs (heart, spleen, liver, lung, and kidney) from mice 7 days after injection. No significant inflammation was observed in any major organ (Fig. S8), which suggested the excellent biocompatibility and safety of CyCDs.

In this work, CyCDs (∼3.11 nm) were formed by a solvothermal reaction using cyanine dyes and 3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride as raw materials, which showed an obvious NIR-II emission from 1100 to 1200 nm with peak centered at 1160 nm. The amine groups on the surface of CyCDs enabled them to rapidly and efficiently bind to bacteria, causing low background fluorescence in PBS buffer, which could achieve high-performance wash-free bacterial imaging. Due to the presence of the efflux pump system on the drug-resistant bacteria, CyCDs can only stain the surface of drug-resistant bacteria, while normal bacteria had a bright fluorescent signal over all of the cell, realizing the identification of drug-resistant bacteria in vitro. The NIR-II fluorescence imaging test demonstrated that the obtained CyCDs allowed long-term detection of the dynamic changes in the bacterial load and reflected relationships between the immune system and bacterial infection, facilitating optimization of antibiotic treatment in clinical practice in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21925802, 22090010 and 22090011), the NSFC-Liaoning United Fund (U1908202), and the Fundamental Research Fundamental Funds for the Central Universities (DUT22LAB601).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144530.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
